Empires & Puzzles Wren Review: 4-Star Nature Rogue Guide

Wren brings a squirrel and a unique chain mechanic to the battlefield, promising to punish buff-heavy teams. But does this Clash of Knights scout still have the stats to compete, or is her bark worse than her bite? We analyze the numbers.
Wren: The Squirrel Scout's Chain Reaction (And Why It Often Fizzles)
Wren is a 4-star Nature (Green) hero from the

Alliance Quest. Released back in late 2021, this scout brings a furry friend to battle—a squirrel that acts as a homing missile for enemies with buffs. As a Rogue, she has the innate ability to dodge special skills, which can be a lifesaver.

While her concept is fun—essentially a "Mini-Finley"—her execution is heavily dependent on the enemy team's setup. She isn't a hero you can plug into any team and expect results; she requires a specific environment to thrive. And yes, despite the confusion in the community, the squirrel doesn't discriminate—it bites everyone equally.

Wren features a charming, slightly whimsical design fitting for a scout. She sports practical leather armor, a heavy backpack indicating she's prepared for the long haul, and of course, her signature squirrel perched atop her head. The art style is consistent with the "knights and castles" theme of her event, leaning more into the adventurous squire trope than the gritty warrior. It’s a lighthearted design in a game often filled with demons and undead warlords.
Let's be blunt: Wren's stats are showing their age. Released in 2021, her base power of
sits significantly lower than modern 4-star heroes who regularly push
This is low by today's standards. Modern 4-star attackers like
(794) start with over 100 more attack points before emblems.
These defensive stats leave her quite squishy. Newer 4-stars often boast 1400-1500+ HP, meaning Wren dies much faster to slash attacks and special skills.
To make her viable in the current meta, Limit Breaking (LB1 and preferably LB2) is almost mandatory to bring her survival up to par.

The damage reduces on each jump. While the card doesn't specify the percentage, community testing suggests a significant drop-off (around 50% less damage per bounce). This means by the 3rd or 4th hit, the squirrel is merely tickling the enemy. Furthermore, hitting a single target for 240% at Fast speed is incredibly weak compared to standard snipers like

Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities

The Raven family bonus offers a chance to make ailments uncleansable. Since Wren's only ailment is Attack Down, this is... okay? Making Attack Down uncleansable can be annoying for the enemy, but it's not game-breaking like uncleansable Mana Control or Defense Down.
is the best possible Aether power for her. Since her base attack is low and her multiplier is mediocre, she needs every point of extra damage she can get to make that first hit count.

Upon release, Wren was seen as a fun "budget Finley." Players were excited about a 4-star with chain mechanics. However, excitement cooled quickly when players realized the damage drop-off was steep and the condition (enemies must have buffs) was harder to satisfy reliably than Defense Down.

4-stars offer far more utility or raw damage.

The key to using Wren is
The most reliable way to use Wren is with
Wilbur casts "Shared Damage" on all enemies.
Shared Damage counts as a buff.
Fire Wilbur first -> Enemies get Defense Down AND Shared Damage (Buff) -> Fire Wren.
Wren sees the buff on the target, hits them, sees the buff on the next, and chains to
. Combined with Wilbur's Defense Down, this turns her into a true AOE nuke.
Ensure you fire Marcel/Almur first. If the enemy has buffs, Wren cleans up.
Wilbur sets the buffs, Wren chains off them, and Caedmon dispels everything afterward.
Wren, you remove the buffs she needs to chain.
Always fire Wren before your dispeller.
While they provide a buff for her to chain off, hitting a Riposte team with a multi-hit chain can make Wren commit suicide instantly.
If you pulled Wren early, she is a decent Fast sniper. Don't worry too much about the chain mechanic; just use her to snipe key targets. However, do not prioritize her over
. They are far more versatile and useful in the long run.

Wren is likely a "bench warmer" for you. Unless you lack depth for 4-star Rush or Buff Booster tournaments, she isn't worth the Limit Break aethers. Her stats are simply too low to survive against modern defenses, and her damage output doesn't justify the slot compared to a costume-boosted Caedmon.
The community has had a love-hate relationship with Wren, mostly centering on her confusing skill text and underwhelming damage.
"Wren sucks balls, he hits like a crippled squirrel."
— A colorful (and common) sentiment regarding her damage output on the tail end of the chain.
"I love her special effect... almost as fun as Grimble."
— Players generally appreciate the visual of the squirrel, even if the damage isn't there.
"Kingston meets Finley... but the Wish.com version."
Many players were initially confused by the text
"If none of the other enemies have buffs, targets a random enemy,"
leading to endless debates on whether she always hits at least twice (She does not; she hits once, and
that target has no buffs, she hits
the chain condition fails immediately, but testing showed she often just stops).
Wren is a hero with a five-star mechanic trapped in a four-star body with three-star damage scaling. The

of a Fast, chaining attacker is fantastic, but the requirement for enemy buffs—combined with the steep damage penalty on bounces—makes her too inconsistent for general play. She is a specialist tool for
tournaments and a fun partner for
, but otherwise, this squirrel is best left gathering nuts in your roster.
Keep one for Buff Booster tournaments, but prioritize standard dispellers and elemental defense down heroes first.
A comprehensive review of Wren, the 4-star Nature Rogue from the Clash of Knights alliance event. We analyze her chain attack mechanics, stats, and best uses in today's meta.
